section.title: Rules for Priests
section.sectionRationale: Following the holiness code for the whole nation in chapters 18 through 20, attention turns specifically to the priests, who must meet a stricter standard fitting their role serving at the altar, touching mourning, marriage, and physical wholeness.
section.thesis: Because priests handle the holy food of God and represent Israel at the altar, God holds them to a stricter standard than the rest of the community regarding contact with the dead, marriage, and physical wholeness, while still including priests with defects within the priestly family and its provision.
section.frameNote: Three movements: ordinary priests and their limits on mourning contact with the dead and marriage; the high priest's even stricter standard; and priestly descendants with physical defects, who share in the priestly food but do not serve at the altar.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses, “Speak to Aaron’s sons, the priests, and tell them that a priest is not to defile himself for a dead person among his people,
except for his immediate family, his mother, father, son, daughter, or brother,
or his unmarried sister who is near to him, since she has no husband.
He is not to defile himself for those related to him by marriage, and so profane himself.
Priests must not make bald spots on their heads, shave off the edges of their beards, or make cuts in their bodies.
They must be {{qadosh|holy}} to their God and not profane the name of their God. Because they present to the LORD the offerings made by fire, the {{lechem|food}} of their God, they must be holy.
A priest must not marry a woman defiled by prostitution or divorced by her husband, for the priest is holy to his God.
You are to regard him as holy, since he presents the food of your God. He shall be holy to you, because I the LORD am holy, I who set you apart.
If a priest’s daughter defiles herself by prostituting herself, she profanes her father; she must be burned in the fire.

units.label: The high priest, held to an even stricter standard
units.note: no loose hair, no torn garments

verses.text: The priest who is highest among his brothers, who has had the anointing oil poured on his head and has been ordained to wear the priestly garments, must not let his hair hang loose or tear his garments.
He must not go near any dead body; he must not defile himself, even for his father or mother.
He must not leave or desecrate the sanctuary of his God, for the {{nezer|consecration}} of the anointing oil of his God is on him. I am the LORD.
The woman he marries must be a virgin.
He is not to marry a widow, a divorced woman, or one defiled by prostitution. He is to marry a virgin from his own people,
so that he does not defile his offspring among his people, for I am the LORD who sanctifies him.”

units.label: Priestly descendants with a physical defect
units.note: provided for, though not serving at the altar

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Say to Aaron, ‘For the generations to come, none of your descendants who has a {{mum|physical defect}} may approach to offer the food of his God.
No man who has any defect may approach, no man who is blind, lame, disfigured, or deformed;
no man who has a broken foot or hand,
or who is a hunchback or dwarf, or who has an eye defect, a festering rash, scabs, or a crushed testicle.
No descendant of Aaron the priest who has a defect shall approach to present the offerings made by fire to the LORD. Since he has a defect, he is not to come near to offer the food of his God.
He may eat the most holy food of his God as well as the holy food,
but because he has a defect, he must not go near the veil or approach the altar, so as not to desecrate My sanctuaries. For I am the LORD who sanctifies them.’”
Moses told this to Aaron and his sons and to all the Israelites.

qadosh.gloss: Holy, set apart. Priests are called to embody this in a stricter, more visible way than the rest of Israel, since they present the food of God directly and represent the whole people before Him.

lechem.gloss: Bread or food, applied here to the offerings the priests present to God. The phrase food of their God frames sacrifice as a meal shared between God and His people, mediated through the priesthood.

nezer.gloss: Consecration, or the crown or mark of dedication, from a root meaning to separate or dedicate. Used here of the anointing oil's mark upon the high priest, and elsewhere of the golden plate on his turban and of the Nazirite's vow, all describing a life visibly set apart.

mum.gloss: A blemish, defect, or physical flaw. Used both of priests barred from altar service and of animals unfit for sacrifice elsewhere in Leviticus, the word ties visible wholeness to the symbolic requirements of approaching a holy God at the altar, without implying anything about the person's worth or standing before Him.

basic.context: This chapter sets a stricter standard for priests than for ordinary Israelites: limits on contact with the dead, restrictions on marriage, and a rule that priestly descendants with a visible physical defect may not personally serve at the altar, though they remain part of the priestly family and share in its food.
basic.hermeneutics: This is old covenant ceremonial regulation for the specific, visible office of altar service under the Mosaic priesthood. It does not describe God's view of a person's worth; the New Testament's picture of Christ deliberately including the blind, lame, and disfigured supersedes this exclusion for the church today.
basic.originalLanguages: Qadosh, holy, and lechem, the food of God, frame the priest's whole calling: he handles what is holy, so he must himself be visibly whole and set apart, nezer. Mum, blemish or defect, ties this visible wholeness to specific altar service, without touching the person's standing before God, since verse 22 explicitly says he still eats the holy food.
basic.typology: The priest barred from the altar by a physical defect, though still fed at God's table, anticipates Christ's welcome of exactly such people, the blind, lame, and disfigured, not merely to a shared meal but to full inclusion in His kingdom. The high priest's total separation from defilement by death anticipates Christ, our great High Priest, holy and undefiled, yet unafraid to touch the dead.
basic.christ: Hebrews 7:26 calls Jesus the high priest we needed: holy, innocent, undefiled, separate from sinners. Where a priest with a defect could not approach the altar, and even a healthy priest had to avoid the dead, Jesus touches the coffin at Nain and the hand of Jairus's dead daughter, and instead of becoming unclean, He gives life.
basic.god: God requires those who represent Him at the altar to embody, visibly, the wholeness fitting His holiness; yet He does not exclude priests with defects from His household or its provision, showing that exclusion from a specific function is not exclusion from belonging or care.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 7:26-28 describes Christ as the perfectly holy High Priest. Luke 7:11-15 shows Jesus touching a coffin and raising the dead without being defiled. Luke 14:13, 21 has Jesus command that the poor, crippled, lame, and blind be invited to the banquet. 1 Peter 2:5, 9 calls all believers a holy priesthood.
basic.oneStory: Israel's priesthood modeled, in visible, physical terms, the wholeness and separation holiness requires, while still caring for those it could not let serve at the altar. Christ, our perfectly whole and holy High Priest, both fulfills that pattern and reverses its exclusions, welcoming the very people it kept from the altar into the fullness of His kingdom.

context.literary: This chapter narrows the holiness code's focus from the whole nation, chapters 18 through 20, to the priesthood specifically, applying a stricter version of the same principle: those who handle what is holy must themselves visibly embody wholeness and separation.
context.historical: Priests in Israel functioned as mediators between a holy God and an often unholy people; their visible conduct, marriage, mourning, and physical fitness for altar service, carried weight for the whole community's access to God, not merely their personal reputation.
context.canonical: This chapter's vocabulary, holiness, consecration, and defects barring approach to the altar, becomes the backdrop against which the New Testament describes Christ as the perfectly holy and physically whole High Priest, and describes His church as a holy priesthood of all believers.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish a stricter standard of visible holiness for the priesthood, fitting their unique role of presenting God's food and mediating Israel's access to Him, while still providing for and including those among them unable to serve at the altar.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is old covenant ceremonial regulation for the specific, visible office of altar service under the Mosaic priesthood; it does not describe God's view of a person's worth, and it prescribes no comparable exclusion for the church, where believers with disabilities serve fully as members of Christ's body, including in leadership.

debates.question: Does the exclusion of priests with physical defects from altar service in verses 17-23 mean God devalues people with disabilities?

views.label: A symbolic requirement for a specific old covenant office, not a judgment on the person's worth
views.case: The passage explicitly says such a priest still eats the holy and most holy food, verse 22, remaining fully within the priestly family and its provision; the restriction concerns visible, symbolic wholeness required specifically for approaching the altar, not the man's worth or standing before God.

views.label: A hard text requiring honest wrestling, not an explanation that removes all its difficulty
views.case: Some acknowledge this text has at times been used to wound people with disabilities, and insist Christians must read it alongside the New Testament's full inclusion of people with disabilities in Christ's own ministry and the church's worship and leadership, refusing to let old covenant ceremonial symbolism outweigh the gospel's welcome.

debates.bearing: Both views converge on the same practical point: whatever this text's original symbolic function within Israel's altar service, it is not a statement about a person's worth before God, and the New Testament's picture of Christ deliberately including the very people this law excluded supersedes it for the church today.

study.originalLanguages: Qadosh, holy, and lechem, the food or bread of God, together frame the priest's calling: since he handles what is holy, he must himself embody visible wholeness and nezer, consecration, the very mark that also names the golden plate on the high priest's turban and the Nazirite's dedicated life. Mum, blemish or defect, ties this required visible wholeness specifically to altar service, without ever touching the excluded priest's place within the priestly family or his share in its food.

typology.type: Priests barred from the altar by physical defect, though still eating the holy food
typology.fulfillment: Reversed in Christ, who welcomes the blind, lame, and disfigured not merely to a shared meal but to full inclusion in His kingdom banquet, Luke 14:13, 21.

typology.type: The high priest's total separation from defilement by death
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, our great High Priest, holy, blameless, and separate from sinners, yet who touches the dead and unclean and is never defiled Himself, Hebrews 7:26, Luke 7:14.

study.christ: Hebrews 7:26 describes exactly the high priest sinners need: holy, innocent, undefiled, separate from sinners, yet exalted above the heavens. Where a priest with a physical defect could not approach the altar, and even a healthy priest had to avoid every contact with a corpse, Jesus touches the coffin of the widow's son at Nain and the hand of Jairus's dead daughter, and instead of becoming unclean Himself, He gives life, showing that in Him the old logic of contagion becomes a logic of healing and welcome.
study.god: God requires those who represent Him at the altar to embody, visibly, the wholeness and separation appropriate to His holiness, since careless or defiled priestly service would profane His name before the whole nation. Yet He does not cast off priests with defects from His household; they still eat His holy food, showing that exclusion from a specific function under the old covenant was never exclusion from belonging or provision.

crossReferences.note: Christ is the perfectly holy, innocent, and undefiled High Priest this chapter's standards for the priesthood anticipate.
Jesus touches a coffin and raises the widow's son at Nain without being defiled, reversing the logic of priestly avoidance of the dead.
Jesus commands that the poor, crippled, lame, and blind be invited to the banquet, welcoming exactly those this chapter excluded from altar service.
All believers are now called a holy priesthood, a chosen people, through union with Christ.

study.oneStory: Israel's priesthood modeled, in visible and physical terms, the wholeness and separation that holiness requires, while still caring for those it could not let serve at the altar. Christ, our perfectly whole and holy High Priest, both fulfills that pattern completely and reverses its exclusions, welcoming the very people it kept from the altar, the blind, the lame, the defiled, into the fullness of His kingdom and His table.

quiz.q: For which relatives could an ordinary priest defile himself by contact with the dead?

options.text: His immediate family: mother, father, son, daughter, brother, or unmarried sister.
options.note: Verses 1-3 list exactly this immediate family exception.

options.text: Any relative at all, without restriction.
options.note: Verses 1-4 explicitly restrict this to immediate family, excluding those related only by marriage.

options.text: No relative whatsoever; priests could never approach the dead.
options.note: Verses 2-3 explicitly permit contact for immediate family.

options.text: Only his own children.
options.note: Verses 2-3 list a broader set of immediate family members, not children alone.

quiz.q: What made the high priest's standard stricter than that of ordinary priests regarding the dead?

options.text: He could not defile himself even for his own father or mother.
options.note: Verse 11 explicitly forbids this even exception granted to ordinary priests.

options.text: He was permitted the same exceptions as ordinary priests.
options.note: Verse 11 explicitly removes the family exception ordinary priests were given.

options.text: He faced no restrictions regarding the dead at all.
options.note: Verse 11 explicitly restricts even this most basic contact for the high priest.

options.text: The text draws no distinction between the high priest and ordinary priests on this point.
options.note: Verses 10-12 explicitly draw this stricter distinction.

quiz.q: Whom was the high priest required to marry, according to verse 13?

options.text: A virgin from his own people.
options.note: Verses 13-14 specify this requirement directly.

options.text: Any widow of his choosing.
options.note: Verse 14 explicitly excludes widows from this requirement for the high priest.

options.text: No requirement is given for the high priest's marriage.
options.note: Verses 13-14 give a specific requirement.

options.text: A foreign woman from a neighboring nation.
options.note: Verse 14 specifies a virgin from his own people, not a foreigner.

quiz.q: What happened to a priestly descendant who had a physical defect, according to verses 21-22?

options.text: He could not approach the altar to offer sacrifices, but he could still eat the holy and most holy food.
options.note: Verses 21-22 state both halves of this provision explicitly.

options.text: He was completely cast out of the priestly family and its provisions.
options.note: Verse 22 explicitly states he retained his share in the priestly food.

options.text: He was required to serve at the altar despite his defect.
options.note: Verse 21 explicitly forbids altar service for such a priest.

options.text: No distinction is made regarding physical defects among priests.
options.note: Verses 17-23 make this distinction explicit and detailed.

quiz.q: How does Hebrews 7:26 describe the High Priest believers actually need?

options.text: Holy, innocent, undefiled, and separate from sinners.
options.note: Hebrews 7:26 uses this exact language of Christ.

options.text: A priest chosen by lineage from Aaron's family alone.
options.note: Hebrews explicitly argues Christ's priesthood is of a different order than Aaron's, not based on physical descent.

options.text: One who must repeatedly offer sacrifices for his own sins first.
options.note: Hebrews 7:27 explicitly contrasts Christ with priests who need to offer for their own sins first.

options.text: Hebrews gives no description of the high priest believers need.
options.note: Hebrews 7:26 gives this description directly.

questions: Priests were held to a stricter standard because of what they handled and represented before God. Where has God given you a particular role or influence that calls for a correspondingly higher level of integrity?

A priest with a physical defect could not serve at the altar, yet he still ate at God's table. Where might you be treating someone's limitation, your own or another's, as if it disqualified them from belonging, when God has not disqualified them from His provision and love?

Jesus touched the dead and the unclean and was never Himself defiled; instead, He gave life. Where do you need to bring your own uncleanness or the brokenness of someone you love directly to Him, rather than keeping a careful distance?

The high priest could not even mourn his own parents in the ordinary way, because of the weight of his calling. What costly sacrifices has following God asked of you, and how do you tend to your grief when duty and sorrow collide?
Christ welcomes the blind, lame, and disfigured not merely to a meal but to full belonging in His kingdom. Who around you needs to hear and see that kind of full welcome from you this week?

translationNotes.note: Bald spots, shaved beard edges, and cuts in the body likely refer to specific pagan mourning rites practiced among Israel's neighbors, which priests especially were forbidden to imitate.

Burned in the fire, applied to a priest's daughter who defiles herself, parallels the penalty given in Leviticus 20:14 for a distinct offense, underscoring how seriously the honor of the priestly family was regarded.

Physical defect translates mum, the same word used elsewhere in Leviticus for a blemish disqualifying an animal from sacrifice, applied here by analogy to the priest's own visible fitness for altar service, not to his standing before God.
